title: Dobos torte
subtitle: Hungarian layered sponge cake with chocolate buttercream and a hard caramel disc
excerpt: A Hungarian celebration cake of six thin sponge layers filled and coated with dark chocolate buttercream, topped with a single brittle caramel disc scored into wedge portions before it sets. The sponge layers are baked individually on baking paper circles, which ensures an even thickness impossible to achieve by splitting a single cake. The caramel disc is poured and scored quickly; once it hardens it cannot be cut without shattering.
sourceNotes: The Dobos torte was created by Hungarian pastry chef József C. Dobos and first presented at the Budapest General Exhibition in 1885. The original recipe was published in the 1906 edition of Dobos's own book and subsequently entered Hungarian culinary literature broadly. The recipe is documented in August Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (first edition 1903, Project Gutenberg), which describes the torte as widely known in continental Europe by that date. The combination of thin baked sponge rounds, chocolate buttercream, and caramel topping is consistent across Hungarian pastry texts from 1890 onwards.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponge layers up to 2 days ahead and store interleaved with baking paper at room temperature. Assemble and fill on the day of serving; add the caramel disc no more than 4 hours before serving as it softens with time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Each sponge round bakes in 8 to 10 minutes until just set and lightly golden at the edges. Watch carefully; thin rounds overbake quickly.
baking.decoratingTechnique: caramel disc scored into wedge portions before setting

recipeTools.notes: two large baking trays for the sponge rounds
use the base to trace 20cm circles on baking paper as templates

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin, brittle sheet of amber caramel poured over the top sponge layer and scored into wedge-shaped portions before it sets. The caramel is cooked to about 165°C (golden amber) then poured quickly over the sponge, spread thinly with an oiled palette knife, and cut into wedge marks within 30 seconds of pouring, before it hardens. Once set, it cannot be cut without cracking unpredictably; the scoring is the only way to achieve clean portions. The cut caramel wedges are sometimes propped at an angle on top of the assembled cake for a decorative effect.

content.text: The defining feature of a Dobos torte is the

that crowns the top layer. The caramel must be scored into wedge portions within 30 seconds of pouring, before it hardens to glass. Six thin sponge rounds are baked individually on traced circles, which gives even layers without the risk of splitting. Work in two or three stages if you have limited oven space.

attrs.prepNote: a few drops of juice only

content.text: Sponge layers: heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Trace six 20 cm circles on baking paper, flip the paper pencil-side down, and lay on baking trays. Whisk the egg yolks with 100g of the sugar and the vanilla until pale and thick. In a separate bowl, whisk the egg whites to soft peaks, then gradually add the remaining 50g of sugar and whisk to firm peaks. Fold the yolk mixture into the whites, then fold in the sifted flour in three additions. Divide into six equal portions (about 110g each). Spread each portion thinly within a traced circle. Bake two or three rounds at a time for 8 to 10 minutes until just set and barely golden at the edges. Cool on racks.

Buttercream: beat the softened butter with the icing sugar until very pale and fluffy, about 5 minutes. Beat in the egg yolks one at a time, then beat in the cooled melted chocolate. Refrigerate for 20 minutes if very soft.

Caramel disc: place the best-looking sponge round on a lightly oiled baking tray. Oil a palette knife. Dissolve the sugar in the water in a small saucepan over medium heat without stirring. Add the lemon juice, increase the heat, and cook to a golden amber caramel (about 165°C on a sugar thermometer). Immediately pour over the reserved sponge round and spread quickly with the oiled knife. While still liquid, score into 10 wedge portions with an oiled knife. The caramel hardens within 1 to 2 minutes.

Assembly: place one sponge round on a serving plate and spread with a thin, even layer of buttercream. Stack the remaining four plain rounds, spreading buttercream between each layer. Use the remaining buttercream to coat the top and sides. Place the caramel-topped disc on last. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving to let the buttercream firm up.
